design_philosophy.core_loop: Build empire from 4000 BC onward through city construction, resource management, technology research, military training, diplomacy, and wonder building. Balance infrastructure, resources, diplomacy, technology, culture, and military power across multiple turns.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Multi-axis strategic tension: growth vs. stability (civil disorder from unhappy citizens), expansion vs. maintenance costs, military might vs. cultural/diplomatic victory, individual city optimization vs. empire-wide resource allocation.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a civilization leader guiding humanity from ancient times through modern era; build iconic wonders, wage strategic wars, advance technology, and achieve victory through military conquest, cultural dominance, diplomatic alliance, or scientific achievement.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Civilization III introduced culture as a new gameplay pillar (absent in predecessors), requiring players to balance cultural influence alongside military/diplomatic/scientific routes. Citizen mood system forces social management alongside economics. Resource splitting (food/production/commerce) creates deliberate strategic friction rather than automatic optimization.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Turn-based pacing allows unlimited decision time. Configurable game difficulty (implied by 'single-player' modes). Player controls citizen allocation, resource splitting (research/tax/luxuries), technology trading strategies, and diplomatic choices affecting neighboring AI civilizations.

progression.upgrade_system: Technology tree divided into four mandatory ages (Ancient, Middle Ages, Industrial, Modern). Technologies unlock units, buildings, wonders, government types, and special bonuses. Non-requisite technologies provide optional enhancements. Technology brokering allows trading with other civilizations.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Prerequisite technology research gates access to new units, city buildings, wonders of the world, and governmental systems. Age advancement requires completing specific technologies within each age.
replayability_hooks: Multiple victory conditions (military, cultural, diplomatic, scientific)
Technology trading and brokering creates different diplomatic paths
Unique Wonders (one-per-game global competition) force strategic prioritization
Citizen mood and civil disorder create dynamic city management challenges
Terrain improvement decisions (irrigation/mines/roads) vary by map layout
Neighboring AI civilizations create unique diplomatic/military scenarios per game

world_building_techniques: Chronological progression from 4000 BC to modern day creates historical arc
Wonders of the World reference real historical achievements (implied by 'Wonders')
Technology tree mirrors actual historical technological advancement
Civilization-specific units and governments (implied) reinforce distinct cultural identities
Cultural ratings create emergent narrative of cultural dominance and influence

faction_indicators: Government type selection signals strategic orientation
Technology tree choices (military vs. cultural vs. scientific focus) telegraph playstyle
Wonder construction priorities reveal civilization values and long-term strategy
Diplomatic treaties and trade agreements show allegiance patterns
danger_signals: Rapid military unit production indicates preparation for war
Technology focus on military techs signals aggressive expansion intent
Refusal of trade or Right of Passage treaties suggests hostile intent
Culture rating growth in border cities indicates territorial pressure
Civil disorder and unrest in cities suggests internal instability or oppression
environmental_storytelling: Wonder construction in specific cities marks cultural/historical significance
Terrain improvement density reveals economic development and resource prioritization
Road networks show trade route emphasis and economic interconnection
Military unit placement and movement indicate defensive vs. offensive posture
City placement density reveals expansion strategy and territorial claims
